title: The Barbecue

jokes.text: Ew. What are you all doing here? / We have a surprise for you. / Judging from the looks on your faces, I don't want it.
jokes.setup: David's family surprises him at his door with excited expressions
jokes.punchline: David's immediate revulsion — the surprise itself is rejected before it's even revealed
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character comedy — David's dread of joy and family interaction is established instantly. The punchline lands before anything has even happened. Deeply character-integrated; only David would preemptively reject a surprise based purely on facial expressions.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh no.

jokes.text: But then Alexis informed us that this is the longest relationship you've ever had! Four whole months, David.
jokes.setup: The family is building up the cookie delivery as a celebration
jokes.punchline: The reveal that four months is David's personal record, said with genuine warmth, which makes it more devastating
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: it roasts David's romantic history, reveals family dynamics (Alexis ratted him out), and lands with the parents' earnest pride making the dig land harder. 'Four whole months' delivered with genuine enthusiasm is the key comedic lever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can already tell by the look on your faces that whatever this surprise is, I'm not going to like it, and I would prefer if we could all just skip ahead to the part where I'm deeply disappointed.

jokes.text: Four whole months, David. How 'bout that?
jokes.setup: The revelation that four months is David's longest relationship
jokes.punchline: Johnny's cheerful, tone-deaf celebration of an objectively low bar, delivered with genuine pride
jokes.explanation: Johnny's earnest pride in a pitifully low bar is the joke — works on character specificity. Simple but functional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it's hideous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's really not good, Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Where did you even find this?

jokes.text: I had a very intimate connection with Tony, for several years. / She was your pen pal, David. / She was in a penitentiary, dear.
jokes.setup: David defensively claims he's had long relationships before — specifically a 'very intimate connection' with Tony for several years
jokes.punchline: Two-stage reveal: Tony was a pen pal, AND she was in prison — each line lands harder than the last
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in escalating punchlines. The setup ('very intimate connection...for several years') sounds like a legitimate counter-argument. The first punchline (pen pal) deflates it. The second (penitentiary) elevates the absurdity to a new level. Three beats, perfectly timed across three characters, each adding information that makes the previous revelation funnier. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific — only David would count a prison pen pal as a relationship. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor; this exceeds it slightly in structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're so proud of you, David. You're in a committed relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, and you're being so responsible about it. We honestly didn't think you had it in you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's like watching a child take their first steps. Except you're a grown man.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We've lowered the bar so considerably for you, sweetheart.

jokes.text: Is he pulling back? / Has he asked you about an open relationship? / Not yet.
jokes.setup: David seems hesitant to invite Patrick to the barbecue; his parents try to diagnose what's wrong with the relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Not yet' — David's qualifier implies open relationships have been a feature of past relationships, delivered with resigned familiarity
jokes.explanation: 'Not yet' is doing enormous comic work in two words. It implies an entire history of open relationship disasters without spelling any of it out. Perfect economy. The casual delivery makes it land even harder.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've been in a committed relationship for two years.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: With who?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Her name is Crystal. She's... we correspond regularly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, is she incarcerated?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not the point.

jokes.text: Carnies are people too.
jokes.setup: David calls his family 'carnies' and says he won't subject Patrick to eating with them
jokes.punchline: Johnny's gentle, sincere defense of carnival workers — accepting the label completely while objecting to the dismissal
jokes.explanation: Johnny not disputing that they are carnies — only that carnies deserve respect — is a funny choice. It's an implied concession played completely straight. Short, punchy, and character-appropriate for Johnny's earnest decency.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is my cookie. Everyone back away from my cookie.

jokes.text: Perhaps it's this nay-saying reticence that caused your past relationships to... / Fall apart. / ...oxidize.
jokes.setup: Moira begins a cutting remark about David's past relationships 'falling apart'
jokes.punchline: Moira corrects Johnny's more straightforward word with 'oxidize' — a bizarrely precise and affected synonym that is simultaneously more pretentious and somehow more apt
jokes.explanation: Moira correcting 'fall apart' with 'oxidize' is a perfect encapsulation of her character's theatrical pretension. 'Oxidize' is funnier than any insult the writers could have crafted because it reveals Moira's worldview — decay as chemistry, not emotion. The word is both wrong and somehow right. Deeply character-integrated; no other character on television would make this substitution. Worthy of 8+ because the originality of the word choice is genuinely surprising.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you cannot come to this barbecue. My family will be there, and they are absolute carnies.

jokes.text: Oh yeah, it's soft. [after David storms out]
jokes.setup: David refuses to bring Patrick to the barbecue and storms off. The family is left with the oversized cookie.
jokes.punchline: Moira's immediate pivot to assessing the cookie's texture — completely indifferent to David's emotional exit
jokes.explanation: A perfect button to the scene. Moira's emotional detachment from David's drama is a running character note, and pivoting to cookie quality is the ideal expression of it. Extremely economical — four words do the whole job.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think you're being very unfair to carnies. They're hard-working people who travel from town to town, setting up rides, games of chance. They have a code, David. A code!

jokes.text: It's a cookie, David, what's the big deal? / First of all, a cookie is always a big deal...
jokes.setup: Patrick dismisses the cookie as trivial
jokes.punchline: David's 'First of all, a cookie is always a big deal' — treating cookies as a matter of serious principle, then pivoting to the actual problem
jokes.explanation: David's immediate 'first of all' defending cookies before addressing the real complaint is a funny character beat — he has priorities, and snacks are one of them. The comedy comes from the genuine conviction he brings to the cookie defense.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Relationships fall apart.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oxidize.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oxidize.

jokes.text: This is the longest relationship you've ever had? Oh, I shoulda got you more than a cookie.
jokes.setup: David reveals it's officially his longest relationship
jokes.punchline: Patrick's sweet/teasing response — the cookie suddenly seems inadequate as a milestone marker
jokes.explanation: A warm joke that functions as both comedy and character development — we see Patrick's affection for David through gentle teasing. The punchline reframes the cookie from 'too much' to 'not enough' in one beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This cookie is incredibly dry.

jokes.text: Okay, well the cookie was almost too much. Figuratively speaking. I ate half of it on the way here.
jokes.setup: Patrick says the cookie was 'almost too much'
jokes.punchline: He immediately confesses he ate half of it on the walk over — undercutting his own sentiment
jokes.explanation: The self-undercut is well-structured: Patrick plays the romantic, then immediately reveals the practical man underneath. The specificity of 'on the way here' makes it funnier than just 'I ate some of it.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not about the cookie, Patrick. It's never about the cookie. When you dismiss something that I've made, you're dismissing me. You're saying that what I do, what I create, what I care about—it doesn't matter to you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And that terrifies me because I've spent my whole life being dismissed, and I finally found someone who I thought understood that about me, and now you're doing the same thing.

jokes.text: If we throw a renaissance fair every month, I just feel like we might be tempting fate.
jokes.setup: David explains why he doesn't want to celebrate monthly anniversaries
jokes.punchline: The jump from 'cookie' to 'renaissance fair' — David's anxiety catastrophizes the escalation of celebrations in a comically specific direction
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'renaissance fair' as David's imagined endpoint for celebration escalation is the joke. It's an absurd but internally logical leap for someone catastrophizing. The 'tempting fate' superstition layered underneath adds character depth.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't understand why we need to celebrate our anniversary every month. That's excessive.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's romantic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a renaissance fair. Every month we're doing a renaissance fair.

jokes.text: The last time I heard that, I was dating a birthday clown who painted my face in the night, and was literally never seen from again.
jokes.setup: Patrick tells David to learn to trust people
jokes.punchline: David's counter-example: a birthday clown who painted his face while he slept and then vanished — a genuinely disturbing story delivered as casual romantic history
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The birthday clown anecdote is a perfect encapsulation of David's chaotic romantic past: absurd premise (dating a birthday clown), dark detail (painted his face while sleeping), and a vanishing that implies something even darker — all delivered by David as if this is a perfectly normal thing to bring up. 'Literally never seen from again' as a casual aside is the masterstroke. This is exceptional — comparable to the 8.0 craft anchor but exceeds it in originality and character integration. The follow-up 'Probably dead' from Patrick makes it even better.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You need to learn to trust people, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I trusted someone once. He said he was a podiatrist. Turns out he was a foot enthusiast with a collection.

jokes.text: He was never found. / Probably dead.
jokes.setup: David says the birthday clown 'was literally never seen from again'
jokes.punchline: David's coda ('he was never found') + Patrick's cheerful 'Probably dead' — two beats that escalate the dark absurdism
jokes.explanation: Patrick's 'Probably dead' is a perfect button — he's completely unfazed, which reveals his growing understanding of David's world. The casualness with which both characters accept the likely death of a birthday clown is the joke. Two words, enormous impact. This earns 8+ because the economy is exceptional and it recontextualizes Patrick's character in one beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Too much? There's no such thing as too much Julia Stiles. She's a consummate professional with range and depth. Have you seen her in the Bourne films? The woman is captivating.

jokes.text: Yeah, who else would I be talking to? No offense.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives at the motel front desk asking if Stevie is talking to her
jokes.punchline: Stevie confirms she was addressing Alexis — the only other person there — while reflexively adding 'no offense' as if insulting whoever else might have been present
jokes.explanation: A small but effective Stevie moment — her dry 'no offense' to an empty room is very on-brand. Not a standout but competent character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: So the clown just... left?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, he's dead now.

jokes.text: So David's at work and you had nobody else to talk to? / What? / That's...
jokes.setup: Alexis says she wants 'girl talk' advice from Stevie
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately cuts to the subtext — Alexis has no other female friends and is here by default
jokes.explanation: Stevie's blunt self-awareness cuts right through Alexis's flattery. The dangling 'That's...' from Alexis, who can't even complete a denial, is a nice comedic beat. Good but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I need to talk to you about something, and I know you're probably not the first person I would normally go to for this, but...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I didn't mean it like that! I just meant that you're not really a 'girl talk' kind of person, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is, I'm your last resort.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? No! I just... oh my God, that sounds so bad.

jokes.text: Okay, so it was an accident. Or, it was made to look like an accident.
jokes.setup: Alexis shows Stevie a 'random' text from her ex Ted saying 'I'm more of a bagel guy, winky face'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's noir-ish pivot — treating a text message as if it were a crime scene
jokes.explanation: The beat-pause between 'accident' and 'made to look like an accident' is doing the comedic work — Stevie applying detective logic to a flirty text is a funny character choice. Good economy, genuine laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, he obviously did it on purpose. I mean, it's basic manipulation. You text someone something mean, and then you immediately follow it up with something nice so they don't know how to feel. It's like, manipulation 101.

jokes.text: It's a tactic, Stevie. You send someone a random text message to get their attention, and then the next thing you know, you're talking again. / Who would do that? / I've done that. Like a lot. / I did it last week.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains the 'accidental text' tactic
jokes.punchline: Her immediate confession that she does this all the time, including last week — self-incriminating with complete unawareness
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'who would do that' to 'I did it last week' in the span of two lines is well-structured. Alexis's lack of self-awareness is the recurring comedic engine and it's working here. 'I did it last week' is the perfect button.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you have to play it cool, but not too cool. You need to text back, but not immediately. And you have to like their Instagram posts, but only the ones from like, three weeks ago, so it doesn't seem like you're stalking them.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Or you could just not date anyone.

jokes.text: This is why I choose not to be in a relationship.
jokes.setup: Alexis casually admits she runs the 'accidental text' game regularly
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry declaration that this confirms her life choices
jokes.explanation: Stevie's resigned conclusion is a good character note and gets a solid laugh. Not particularly original but well-placed and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I had Zac Efron on call for sex.

jokes.text: I used to text Zac Efron just like a question mark whenever I wanted a booty call. Poor thing would be like buzzing my apartment before I even pressed send.
jokes.setup: Rachel mentions texting a 'bunch of letters' to get someone's attention
jokes.punchline: Alexis one-ups with a celebrity booty call system — Zac Efron responding to a question mark so fast he arrived before she sent it
jokes.explanation: Alexis's casual celebrity name-dropping has a long history in the show, and this is a solid instance. 'Poor thing would be like buzzing my apartment before I even pressed send' is the payoff — the physical comedy implied by a frantic Zac Efron sprinting to her door is vivid. The 'poor thing' adds a layer of Alexis's oblivious condescension.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you look so good. Like, I'm actually genuinely impressed. You know, for you.

jokes.text: You're like super smart, and pretty in like a... breezy, non-threatening sort of way.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to compliment Stevie for her girl-talk advice
jokes.punchline: 'Non-threatening' — a backhanded compliment that reveals more about Alexis's competitive beauty standards than it does about Stevie
jokes.explanation: A beautifully delivered backhanded compliment. The pause before 'breezy, non-threatening sort of way' is doing the work — Alexis is genuinely trying to be kind and doesn't hear herself. It's cringe comedy that reveals character rather than simply embarrassing anyone. 'Non-threatening' is the perfect word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't even imagine you cooking. The last time you were in the kitchen, you thought the convection oven was a piece of modern art.

jokes.text: Another successful girl talk. [Stevie's reaction after Alexis leaves]
jokes.setup: Alexis has just called Stevie 'non-threatening' as a compliment and walked out
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat, sardonic declaration to no one — the audience infers her expression of pained acceptance
jokes.explanation: A perfect Stevie button line. The irony of 'successful' after being called non-threatening is well-placed. Short, dry, character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I'm a grill master. Look at these aprons I've collected over the years.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This one says 'Grill, Baby, Grill.' This one says 'I'm on Fire.' And this one says 'Let's Meat.'

jokes.text: You know, Roland, when you said you found a grill, this isn't exactly what I had in mind. / What'd you expect Johnny, Benihana?
jokes.setup: Johnny expected a proper grill; Roland has produced something clearly inadequate
jokes.punchline: Roland's 'Benihana' comeback — a complete non sequitur as a defensive deflection that somehow works
jokes.explanation: A solid fish-out-of-water beat. Roland's Benihana deflection is funny because it's both absurd and vaguely on-point (it IS a restaurant with a grill). Competent but not remarkable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got you something. It's a apron. It says 'I'm on a roll' with a picture of a hotdog on it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's... that's very thoughtful, but I'm not sure an apron is going to solve my problems with the grill.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, the thing is, Johnny, it's not about the grill. It's about the man behind the grill. And right now, that man needs to know he's on a roll.

jokes.text: Do you know that I've been given three different aprons because of my barbecue skills? Let's see, I've got License to Grill, I've got Working Grill, oh and I have Grills Just Wanna Have Fun.
jokes.setup: Roland defends his grill master credentials
jokes.punchline: Three pun aprons listed with complete earnest pride — 'Grills Just Wanna Have Fun' is the escalating third
jokes.explanation: The rule of three is well-applied here with proper escalation — 'License to Grill' is fine, 'Working Grill' is better (unexpected Dolly Parton pivot), 'Grills Just Wanna Have Fun' is the best because it combines Cyndi Lauper AND implies Roland has a whole collection system. Roland's pride makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where were you? I've been worried sick. Were you kidnapped? Did you get lost? Were you trapped in a well? Did you join a cult? Are you in witness protection?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I was just stuck in traffic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's a relief. I was about to check the black market.

jokes.text: Okay look, um, I can smell your fear, okay? So why don't you go grab the meat and the briquettes, and I'll get this bad boy up and running for ya?
jokes.setup: Johnny is visibly skeptical of Roland's grill mastery
jokes.punchline: Roland confidently identifying Johnny's fear while himself being completely unqualified — the confidence is the joke
jokes.explanation: Roland's unfounded confidence is a reliable character note. Functional but not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Did you actually search for me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I considered it. Mostly the dramatic rescue part.

jokes.text: I've got the perfect apron for ya, Poor Little Rich Grill. I'll get it made up.
jokes.setup: Johnny claims he's watched personal chefs grill, explaining why he doesn't know how himself
jokes.punchline: Roland has a custom apron concept ready for Johnny's specific class-based ineptitude — and offers to commission it
jokes.explanation: A callback that elevates the original joke. 'Poor Little Rich Grill' is an excellent pun that also skewers Johnny's class position — 'Poor Little Rich Girl' applied to his barbecue helplessness. The extra beat of 'I'll get it made up' implies Roland is seriously going to have this manufactured, which extends the absurdity. This earns 8+ because it's both a strong pun AND a pointed character observation AND a callback that improves on the setup.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What did I do in a past life to deserve this? Was I a vampire?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A vampire?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Or a fitness instructor.

jokes.text: David, someone's ears must be a-flame.
jokes.setup: Patrick walks in while they'd been discussing him
jokes.punchline: Moira uses the antiquated 'ears burning' idiom but with her characteristic theatrical flair — 'a-flame' instead of 'burning'
jokes.explanation: A minor Moira verbal affectation. 'A-flame' instead of burning is a small but consistent character note. Not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What did I do in a past life to deserve this? Was I a vampire? A fitness instructor?

jokes.text: When you said that you were coming back, you just meant that you weren't coming back, and that I would have to spend the afternoon searching for you on the train tracks, and among various unmarked vans.
jokes.setup: Patrick is late returning from town hall; David is upset
jokes.punchline: David's catastrophizing imagines Patrick among 'unmarked vans' — his anxiety leaping to the most dramatic possible missing-person scenarios
jokes.explanation: David's anxiety spiral manifesting as true-crime imagery is very character-specific. 'Various unmarked vans' is the killer detail — the plural 'various' implies a whole network of danger he'd have to comb through. Consistent with the birthday clown joke's dark undertone in his relationship history.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that's horrifying.

jokes.text: You did that for me? / No, but there was a moment when I thought about doing it.
jokes.setup: Patrick is moved that David would have searched for him on train tracks
jokes.punchline: David's immediate correction — he only briefly considered it, and honesty wins out over romance
jokes.explanation: This is a beautiful David joke — romantic enough to be touching, then immediately undercut by his own honesty. 'There was a moment' is perfect: it's not a lie and it's not a full commitment. Very character-integrated; this is exactly how David expresses affection. The economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, he sees me? Like, all of me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: I don't know what kind of carnage I inflicted in a past life, to deserve it. I must've been... Dracula, or a spin instructor.
jokes.setup: David describes his string of relationship disasters as 'very bad luck'
jokes.punchline: His two guesses for his past-life crime: Dracula (legendary villain) OR spin instructor — the latter being the funnier, more specific horror
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: Dracula sets up the expectation of a famous monster/villain, then 'spin instructor' arrives as the punchline — David considers them equivalent levels of evil. It works because it's genuinely witty (spin instructors ARE widely disliked) AND completely character-specific (David's aesthetic snobbery extending to workout culture). Highly quotable. This earns 8.5+ because the juxtaposition is genuinely surprising and the joke reveals character while being structurally tight.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I need to be very clear about something. A barbecue is for eating meat and vegetables that have been cooked over an open flame. It is not a venue for musical performances. If I see you with that guitar, I will throw it into the pool.

jokes.text: And what if we could finally tie a sailor's knot in that string of bad luck?
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to be encouraging about David's relationship
jokes.punchline: The mixed metaphor — you 'tie' a knot and 'end' a string, but 'sailor's knot' is an odd upgrade that implies she might make it worse
jokes.explanation: A minor Moira malapropism/mixed metaphor moment. It's subtle enough that it might not land for all audiences — it works as character texture more than as a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, hi! How are you?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm good, I'm good. How are you?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm great, I'm great. It's so good to see you.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, so good to see you too.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: How have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You know, can't complain. How about you?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, same, same. Really good.

jokes.text: We're not doing Pat. / He sees you. For all that you are.
jokes.setup: Moira calls Patrick 'sweet Pat' as a nickname
jokes.punchline: David immediately policing the nickname while Moira completes her sentimental point, completely ignoring him
jokes.explanation: The 'Pat' nickname running gag starts here. David's insistence on 'we're not doing Pat' while his mother bulldozes past it is a good dynamic. This is the setup for later callbacks.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Why didn't you just text me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I was going to, but then I thought about how you always respond better to things when I show up in person, and I remembered that time I 'accidentally' texted you that thing, and you came running over, so I figured I'd just come by instead.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Wait, that was on purpose?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Obviously.

jokes.text: Well, hopefully not all that I am, I mean that would be...
jokes.setup: Moira says Patrick 'sees David for all that he is'
jokes.punchline: David's nervous clarification — hoping Patrick hasn't actually seen EVERYTHING he is, trailing off as if the full picture would be too much
jokes.explanation: A good self-deprecating beat for David — acknowledging that full transparency about himself would be alarming. The trailing off is the right choice; spelling it out would kill it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'd like to eat at 8 o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: 8 o'clock? That's dinner time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, that's when I want to eat dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: At night?

jokes.text: Fine. He can come. / Excellent. And he's bringing his guitar. / What?
jokes.setup: David reluctantly agrees to invite Patrick to the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately reveals the guitar detail — she's already planned this completely, and the guitar is a step too far for David
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works: David concedes, thinks it's over, then the guitar arrives as a new threat. Moira's pre-planning is revealed in one word. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh God, oh God. Where's the... how does this... David! David, get out here!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is a wide shot of a man losing his mind over a barbecue. Very cinematic. Very Bergman.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The lighting is all wrong. Why is everything on fire? This is not the three-point lighting we discussed!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I need a close-up on the panic. Get in tight. The audience needs to see the fear in my eyes.

jokes.text: ...in case there's a rousing fireside sing-along? / No, I draw the line at sing-along.
jokes.setup: The guitar is explained as preparation for a potential sing-along
jokes.punchline: David has a hierarchy of acceptable barbecue activities; sing-along is below his threshold
jokes.explanation: 'Rousing fireside sing-along' is funny phrasing from Johnny, and David's firm 'I draw the line' is a good character note. Functional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you accomplished that all by yourself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you, Mother.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's quite impressive for someone of your... limitations.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not sure that's the compliment you think it is.

jokes.text: Alexis and Ted's entire awkward scene where neither can admit they texted intentionally
jokes.setup: Alexis shows up at Ted's work having advised Stevie that direct communication is better than games — she then proceeds to play every game possible
jokes.punchline: The sustained irony of their mutual denial — both knowing the text was intentional, neither able to say it
jokes.explanation: The entire scene functions as an extended irony joke — Alexis lectured Stevie about game-playing minutes before demonstrating every game in the book. The comedic structure (advice → immediate contradiction) is excellent. Each line of denial adds to the cringe. Scored as a unit because it's a sustained comedic mechanism rather than individual beats.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, but you're also like, aggressively average-looking.

jokes.text: What, uh, what'd it say? / Nothing. I don't think it said anything, now that I think about it.
jokes.setup: Ted asks what the text said; Alexis just mentioned it to him
jokes.punchline: Alexis backtracks in real time — the text she brought up now 'said nothing'
jokes.explanation: A specific beat within the larger Ted/Alexis cringe sequence. Alexis retroactively erasing the content of a text she literally described is absurd and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Pat, come here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please don't call me that.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? Pat? Your name is Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but I go by David. Patrick is my father's name, and I have no desire to be called Pat. It's not happening.

jokes.text: Hello dummy, why do you think I'm here? I'm here to tell you to be careful because with those texts, those texts in the wrong hands... / Yeah, it was actually meant for Heather.
jokes.setup: Alexis calls Ted 'dummy' for not realizing why she came — then her reason completely collapses when she realizes he did send it accidentally
jokes.punchline: Her whole cover story evaporates; the grand gesture was for nothing, and she has to accept it
jokes.explanation: The misdirection lands: Alexis confidently explains her plan and it immediately falls apart. The awkwardness of both needing to pretend 'Heather' and 'bagels' make sense is well-played. Character-specific cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your relationship with Patrick is absolutely titillating.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please stop saying that word.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? Titillating? I find it to be a perfectly cromulent descriptor. Titillating, titillating, titillating.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you're doing it on purpose now.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm simply enjoying the word. It has a delightful mouth feel.

jokes.text: Uh no, I should get outta your hair. But it was good to see you, Mr. Bagel. / That's a you-and-Heather thing, but now that you texted me, it's kind of like an all-of-us thing.
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to leave gracefully; Ted has latched onto the bagel nickname
jokes.punchline: Ted insisting 'Mr. Bagel' is now a shared reference because she texted him — the nickname lives beyond its cover story
jokes.explanation: Ted's logic that 'now that you texted me, it's kind of an all-of-us thing' is sweet and funny — he's claiming the accidental intimacy. 'Mr. Bagel' as a parting nickname that refuses to die is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, you were engaged?

jokes.text: Where are we, Barcelona?
jokes.setup: Johnny says he'd like to eat around 8pm
jokes.punchline: Roland's European dinner-time reference — a surprisingly worldly observation from Roland, who isn't typically cosmopolitan
jokes.explanation: Three words that do maximum work. The Spain reference is unexpected from Roland. Perfect economy. Gets a solid chuckle.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? How did we go from discussing the wine list to this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is insane. Moments ago we were talking about the 2015 Bordeaux.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And now you're telling me... this? How is that even possible?

jokes.text: Oh, Jocelyn and I have another one of those birthing rehearsals we gotta go to. / I... just bought $100 worth of sliders.
jokes.setup: Roland, who promised to grill, announces he can't come to the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Johnny's plaintive '$100 worth of sliders' — the money is the injury, the abandonment is the insult
jokes.explanation: Johnny's financial panic at the slider revelation is a nice character note — even now, after losing the Rose fortune, $100 on sliders represents a commitment. The specificity of sliders makes it funnier than 'food.'

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, I love you. I want to spend the rest of my life with you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's very sweet, but have you seen Downton Abbey? Because that's really the gold standard.

jokes.text: Right, cut to. / Well, we're not cutting to. / Cut to, I've ruined my sliders! / Cut to, how does this thing work?
jokes.setup: Roland tells Johnny to 'cut to' the inevitable barbecue disaster
jokes.punchline: Johnny refuses the narrative shortcut, then immediately proves Roland right in two successive 'cut to' moments
jokes.explanation: A structurally excellent joke. Roland's 'cut to' is almost meta-comedic — he's predicting the scene as if narrating a show. Johnny's denial is immediately undercut by the reality Roland predicted, then Roland claims it with more 'cut to' declarations. The escalating 'cut tos' are very funny because Roland isn't there to say them, yet Johnny ironically proves him right. This earns 8+ for the originality of the 'cut to' mechanism used as both character comedy and structural joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I'm fine. Actually, you know what? I'm not fine. I would like a half-caf oat milk latte with a shot of vanilla, but not the vanilla syrup, actual vanilla, and can you make sure the milk is steamed to exactly 150 degrees? And I'll have a everything bagel, but I'd like the everything seasoning removed and replaced with just sea salt. Oh, and some egg whites, but they need to be from free-range, non-GMO chickens, and they should be scrambled, but not too much. Just until they're barely set.

jokes.text: You guys want me to jump in? / No, I think we've got it covered. / Oh, you do, oh great, 'cause I'm gonna get another beer.
jokes.setup: Roland offers to help with the grill
jokes.punchline: The instant pivot to getting a beer the moment he's told he's not needed — the offer was performative
jokes.explanation: Roland's fake helpfulness is a reliable character note. The speed of his pivot to the beer is the key timing element. Solid but familiar.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, he's on his second plate of food, so I'd give it another 20 minutes.

jokes.text: I'd say that one looks about perfect. / I was gonna say the same thing. / Specifically which one were you talking about? / Just this one, here. / Oh yeah, that's the one.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Patrick are managing the grill together; they begin complimenting a burger
jokes.punchline: The elaborate, diplomatic exchange where neither man will admit they're lost — both claiming to be talking about the same (probably wrong) burger with equal confidence
jokes.explanation: A charming scene of two men performing competence for each other. The fact that they're definitely looking at different burgers (or the wrong one) is implied through the overly careful 'specifically which one' exchange. Good character work for both Johnny and Patrick.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, are you okay? You've been in here all day.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm fine, Alexis. Just watching TV.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, my phone is almost dead and I need to charge it in here. Can you unplug whatever you're using?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not using anything.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but I really need an outlet, so...

jokes.text: Okay, should I save him? / Oh they'll be fine. / Let's just sit back and enjoy the sight of our two strapping men bonding over an open flame.
jokes.setup: Moira and Jocelyn watch Johnny and Patrick at the grill with varying levels of competence
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'two strapping men bonding over an open flame' — a romanticized description of what is clearly an anxious muddle
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical reframing of incompetence as masculine ritual is a good character note. 'Strapping men' applied to Johnny Rose trying to work a vintage grill is the irony.

jokes.text: Which is shocking because as you may or may not know, I've struggled with group work in the past.
jokes.setup: Moira asks how the business is going; David says it's thriving
jokes.punchline: David's self-aware admission that group work is a known personal failing — delivered with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: David's self-diagnosis delivered as offhand caveat is very character-specific. The 'as you may or may not know' implies it's a documented fact about him. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: David, you are living an exciting new chapter in the book on tape of your life.
jokes.setup: Moira tries to compliment David on his business success and relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Book on tape' — the detail implies David's life story, while interesting, is best consumed passively at highway speed, or by people who can't be bothered to sit and read it
jokes.explanation: A quietly brilliant Moira turn of phrase — she's being complimentary but 'book on tape' subtly diminishes the story (tapes are for people who won't read). Only Moira would use this phrasing as a sincere compliment. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Okay, just so you know, that's not really a compliment, but thank you.
jokes.setup: Moira tells David he's in a new chapter of his 'book on tape'
jokes.punchline: David catches the implied diminishment but accepts it anyway — his politeness winning out over his need to correct
jokes.explanation: David's 'just so you know, that's not really a compliment, but thank you' is a perfect beat — he notes the flaw, refuses to elaborate, and accepts the gift. The courtesy after the correction is the funny part.

jokes.text: Okay, just so you know, that's not really a compliment, but thank you.
jokes.setup: Moira says David 'did this all by yourself' as praise
jokes.punchline: David immediately identifies that 'did it all by yourself' implies he usually needs help or can't manage things — and quietly calls it out while still accepting it
jokes.explanation: David's radar for backhanded praise is tuned perfectly, and his 'not really a compliment, but thank you' response is a tight, character-specific beat. The 'but thank you' especially — he can't fully reject even a bad compliment.

jokes.text: We can be each other's little... little daties.
jokes.setup: Alexis invites a sad stranger to her parents' barbecue as a gesture of kindness
jokes.punchline: She caps the invitation with 'little daties' — a diminutive, Alexis-specific portmanteau of 'dates' that somehow makes the gesture more sweet and more absurd simultaneously
jokes.explanation: 'Daties' is a perfect Alexis-ism — diminutive, cutesy, and uniquely hers. The repetition ('little... little daties') suggests she's coining the term in real time. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: There ya go, we make a good team, Pat. / No, John, no, we're not doing Pat. / No we're not.
jokes.setup: Johnny uses the 'Pat' nickname David has forbidden
jokes.punchline: David and Patrick simultaneously reject 'Pat' — and then Patrick clarifies he agrees about 'Pat' but also about the team thing
jokes.explanation: The running gag of 'Pat' pays off nicely when Patrick himself rejects it alongside David. The ambiguity of Patrick's 'No we're not' (referring to both 'Pat' AND the team claim) is a subtle double-meaning that rewards attention.

jokes.text: My first job in high school was actually at a Rose Video. / Get outta town! What branch? / 785. / 785. Impressive late fees.
jokes.setup: Patrick reveals he worked at a Rose Video in high school
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate 'Get outta town,' followed by 'What branch' — treating this like a military regiment — then 'Impressive late fees' as the institutional compliment
jokes.explanation: This joke works on multiple levels simultaneously: it's a great callback to the Rose family's lost empire, it's Johnny treating a video store branch number with the pride of a military unit, and 'Impressive late fees' as the highest Rose Video compliment is genuinely excellent. The exchange reveals Johnny's relationship to his former identity and foreshadows the Patrick-Rose connection. Earns 8+ because it's funny, character-specific, AND narratively important — all at once.

jokes.text: Well, let's hope that you continue to surprise each other. It keeps the relationship titillating. / Okay, please never say titillating when referring to my relationship. / Oh, can I though? / No.
jokes.setup: Moira offers relationship wisdom with an unfortunately chosen word
jokes.punchline: David's immediate veto of 'titillating,' Moira's hopeful follow-up question, and David's flat 'No'
jokes.explanation: The three-beat exchange (veto, hopeful query, firm no) is perfectly structured. Moira's 'Oh, can I though?' as a genuine follow-up rather than a rhetorical question is the funny part — she actually wants to use 'titillating' in this context. Good mother-son dynamic.

jokes.text: Wait, Patrick is your fiancé? / Uh, I'm sorry you have a fiancée?
jokes.setup: Rachel arrives at the barbecue; Patrick and Rachel recognize each other
jokes.punchline: The double revelation landing almost simultaneously — Alexis realizing Rachel is Patrick's ex, David realizing Patrick was engaged
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of Alexis having befriended Patrick's ex-fiancée is excellent episode construction, and the comedic delivery of both revelations in rapid succession gives the scene its impact. David's disbelief is perfectly calibrated. High impact because it's the episode's dramatic and comedic pivot.

jokes.text: Patrick? / Rachel, what are you doing here? / What are you doing here? I've been texting you for 2 days.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives with Rachel as her 'datie'; all seems well until Rachel and Patrick recognize each other
jokes.punchline: The collision of storylines — Rachel is Patrick's ex-fiancée, the very Rachel who's been reaching out to him — lands as a comedic bomb that turns immediately serious
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of multiple converging storylines is excellent writing. The audience has been watching all the pieces set up (Rachel's texts to Patrick, Alexis befriending a stranger, the barbecue) and the collision is earned, surprising, and perfectly timed. The comedic horror of the situation (Alexis brought Patrick's ex-fiancée to his anniversary barbecue) is both funny and dramatically sharp. The best structural joke in the episode.

jokes.text: No I... I mean I don't... now. / But yes, at some point I mean we... we were...
jokes.setup: David asks if Patrick has a fiancée
jokes.punchline: Patrick's fractured, collapsing syntax as he tries to accurately describe the status of his engagement
jokes.explanation: Patrick's inability to form a sentence about his romantic history is funny because it's so un-Patrick — he's usually measured and calm. The crumbling syntax reveals his panic and guilt simultaneously.

jokes.text: I'm gonna talk to you in a minute, we've got a lot to talk about. / Who is she? / I glanced down at my plate for two seconds.
jokes.setup: Chaos breaks out at the barbecue when Rachel appears; Patrick is juggling two conversations
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I glanced down at my plate for two seconds' — she missed the entire dramatic reveal and is completely lost
jokes.explanation: Moira's comic obliviousness to the drama is well-timed. Her focus on her plate during a relationship crisis is perfectly character-consistent. The line is short and does all its work in nine words.

jokes.text: I glanced down at my plate for two seconds.
jokes.setup: Chaos erupts at the barbecue with the Rachel revelation; Moira is suddenly in the middle of it
jokes.punchline: Moira's bewildered, self-exculpatory reaction — she missed the entire dramatic turn by briefly looking at her plate
jokes.explanation: A perfect comic reaction in eight words. Moira's injured innocence — she was simply eating — is funny and character-specific. The implication that turning away from the Rose family for two seconds leads to chaos is a reliable comedic premise.

jokes.text: Whoa. / Over the past few months? / And you didn't think to tell me about this?
jokes.setup: Patrick explains Rachel has been reaching out expecting them to get back together 'for the past few months'
jokes.punchline: David's escalating 'Whoa' — a single word processing a genuinely alarming revelation — before the more serious confrontation
jokes.explanation: David's processing 'Whoa' before launching into the real confrontation is a small comedic beat within a dramatic scene. The comedy arises from his brief struggle to find words before finding better words.

jokes.text: You stood in front of me and told me to trust people. I know. When I was perfectly fine not trusting people. Not trusting people is what I'm used to. It is my comfort zone.
jokes.setup: David confronts Patrick about withholding information about Rachel
jokes.punchline: David's ironic re-embrace of his trust issues — Patrick convinced him to be vulnerable and now David is claiming he was better off paranoid
jokes.explanation: This lands as both comedy and genuine character drama. David's articulation that 'not trusting people is my comfort zone' is funny because it's true, delivered with wounded pride, and it's also a real line for the character. The comedy comes from his self-awareness about his own defenses.

jokes.text: That is quite possibly one of the most beautiful things I've ever heard anyone say. Um... outside of the Downton Christmas Special.
jokes.setup: Patrick delivers a genuinely heartfelt declaration about David making him feel 'right'
jokes.punchline: David's sincere emotional response is immediately qualified by the Downton Abbey Christmas Special — implying a TV episode moved him more than a real human declaration of love
jokes.explanation: A beautiful comedic moment that earns its 8+ score. David is genuinely moved — and immediately cannot help himself from ranking Patrick's declaration against Downton Abbey. The specificity of 'Downton Christmas Special' (not just 'Downton Abbey,' but a specific holiday episode) is perfect. It's deeply character-integrated (David's cultural obsessions competing with his emotions), structurally excellent (the pause before the qualification is everything), and highly quotable. The joke also functions as emotional protection — David is afraid to fully accept the moment so he defuses it with pop culture. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Actually um, I haven't had dinner yet, so... / I'll grab ya a slider. / More than one, um, and some potato salad, and I think there were some other sides on the table, but I couldn't see, so maybe just a smattering of everything.
jokes.setup: After a painful emotional conversation, David asks Patrick to stay
jokes.punchline: Patrick offers a slider as a peace offering; David escalates to a full comfort-food order — 'a smattering of everything' — as emotional reconciliation
jokes.explanation: A warm resolution joke. David's escalating food order as emotional vocabulary is quintessentially David — food as comfort, as forgiveness, as love language. 'A smattering of everything' is both an absurd food request and a touching extension of trust. The callback to the cookie's importance (cookies are always a big deal) adds resonance.

jokes.text: I talked to Tony yesterday, he seemed very, very upset.
jokes.setup: The family gathers after the Patrick-David drama; Moira brings up Tony
jokes.punchline: The prison pen pal Tony is apparently a real ongoing relationship in Moira's life — she checks in on Tony
jokes.explanation: A callback that elevates the original joke significantly. The reveal that Moira personally maintains the pen pal relationship with Tony (the prisoner David counted as a relationship) adds a delightful layer of absurdity and raises new questions. 'He seemed very, very upset' implies regular communication and genuine concern. A late-episode callback that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Okay, don't get me wrong, I feel super bad, and partially responsible because I invited Rachel to the barbecue, but at the same time, he's been watching that show for 3 hours and my phone's in there.
jokes.setup: The family discusses David being upset in his room after the Patrick revelation
jokes.punchline: Alexis's empathy runs approximately 10 seconds before being overridden by phone withdrawal
jokes.explanation: Alexis's character note of limited emotional range is well-deployed here. The 'but at the same time' is the pivot from sister to phone addict. The acknowledgment of her partial responsibility followed immediately by her real concern (the phone) is honest and funny.

jokes.text: Well, you might have to go one night without your phone, Alexis. / Ugh!
jokes.setup: Alexis reveals she needs her phone from David's room
jokes.punchline: Alexis's extended, visceral 'Ugh!' at the suggestion of one phoneless night — disproportionate to the request
jokes.explanation: Functional character reaction but fairly predictable. Alexis's phone-dependence is well-established and the punchline is expected. The reaction itself (Ugh!) is the weakest kind of comedic response — not wrong, just not particularly crafted.

jokes.text: Well, you might have to go one night without your phone, Alexis. / Ugh!
jokes.setup: Alexis wants her phone back from David's room; Johnny suggests she go without it
jokes.punchline: Alexis's anguished 'Ugh!' — a single syllable conveying genuine suffering at the prospect
jokes.explanation: A minor character note for Alexis. The 'Ugh' is perfectly delivered but the joke itself is predictable — Alexis hating to be without her phone is well-established. Functional.

tmdb_overview: David and Moira hold a party for Patrick, but David worries about how his family would adversely affect Patrick. Alexis investigates with help a mysterious text from Ted.
insight: Social anxiety carries the episode—David's discomfort peaks in four consecutive scene shifts.
